masthead.title_html: Reason Your Way Through 3D Printing, Vol. 2
masthead.title_plain: Reason Your Way Through 3D Printing, Vol. 2
masthead.dek: A fresh round on additive manufacturing — the processes and materials, plus the history, pioneers, and real-world uses. Each answer is reached by reasoning, and the tech is often older than you'd guess. Narrow it down, place your bet, then tap to check.
masthead.footer_html: Built fresh against the existing 3D-printing set. Verified to Wikipedia, patents, ASTM, NASA/ESA, and trade sources. A niche topic — this is close to its honest ceiling.

dimensions: how a layer is exposed to light
first-layer helper that does not touch the part
fully melting vs partially fusing metal powder
plan.note: Two branches (technology/processes/materials, history/pioneers/applications), each with a reflex-defeater, fresh dimensions.

questions.q: One powder-based process needs no support structures at all, because the loose powder around each layer holds the part up while it builds. Which process is it?
questions.detail: In SLS a laser fuses nylon powder layer by layer, and the surrounding unfused powder supports overhangs, so no printed supports are required.
questions.why: The reflex is to name a no-support trick, but the question wants a whole process; among the named methods only powder-bed sintering is inherently self-supporting, unlike FDM or resin printing that both need supports.
questions.more: This is why SLS can make moving, interlocking parts in a single build that other methods cannot.
questions.source: Formlabs, 'What is Selective Laser Sintering' (formlabs.com/blog/what-is-selective-laser-sintering); 3D Systems SLS overview (3dsystems.com/selective-laser-sintering)

questions.cat: 3D printing · how a layer is exposed to light
questions.dimension: how a layer is exposed to light
questions.q: Two resin processes both harden liquid with light. One uses a projector to flash a whole layer at once instead of a moving laser dot. Which one flashes the full layer?
questions.detail: DLP projects a 2D image of the entire cross-section so the whole layer cures in one flash, while SLA traces each layer point by point with a laser.
questions.why: Both are resin methods, so the giveaway is the curing motion; the slicer is 'whole layer at once,' which is DLP's projector, not SLA's scanning laser.
questions.more: Because it exposes a full layer per flash, DLP print time depends on height, not on how much area each layer covers.
questions.source: Markforged, 'What is Digital Light Processing (DLP)' (markforged.com); Stratasys, 'DLP 3D Printing Explained' (stratasys.com)

questions.q: On most desktop resin printers the part is built hanging upside down and is slowly lifted up out of a shallow tank of liquid. What is this build setup called?
questions.detail: In a bottom-up resin printer the light comes from below the tank, layers cure on the underside of the plate, and the part is pulled upward out of the resin as it grows.
questions.why: People picture a part rising up out of a deep vat, but consumer resin printers invert it; the answer is the orientation name, and 'upside down, lifted out of a shallow tank' points only to bottom-up.
questions.more: An upside-down build means the tank can be shallow because part height is no longer limited by resin depth.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'Stereolithography' (bottom-up vs top-down); Anycubic 3D printing guides (store.anycubic.com)

questions.q: One common filament shrinks so much as it cools that it can warp or crack unless the printer is boxed in to hold the heat. Which plastic is it?
questions.detail: ABS shrinks a lot while cooling, so a draft or open room can lift its corners; an enclosed, warm chamber keeps it from warping.
questions.why: The trap is to grab any 'tough' plastic, but the slicer is high shrinkage that demands an enclosure; PLA and PETG print fine in open air, while ABS is the classic enclosure case.
questions.more: Finished ABS parts can be smoothed glossy with acetone vapor, a trick that works because acetone dissolves ABS.
questions.source: Prusa Knowledge Base, 'ABS' (help.prusa3d.com/article/abs_2058); Wevolver, 'ABS print temperature considerations'

questions.cat: 3D printing · full-color capability in one build
questions.q: Most printers make a part in a single color. Which powder-and-glue process can lay down full color across the whole part by mixing colored binders as it prints?
questions.detail: Full-color binder jetting spreads gypsum-like powder and glues it with CMYK colored binders jetted from inkjet heads, coloring each spot as it bonds.
questions.why: The reflex is to think 'paint it after,' but the question wants color produced during printing; among powder methods only binder jetting jets colored glue, so it colors as it builds.
questions.more: The colored-binder approach is why full-color figurine prints are usually made in sandstone-like gypsum.
questions.source: Dassault Systemes, 'Binder Jetting' (3ds.com/make/guide/process/binder-jetting); Shapeways, 'Binder Jetting 3D Printing'

questions.q: On a two-nozzle FDM printer, one special material is printed as the support and then simply rinsed away in water afterward. Which material is it?
questions.detail: PVA (polyvinyl alcohol) dissolves in water, so it is printed as support on a second nozzle and washed off, leaving the model untouched.
questions.why: The trap is to name a model plastic; the slicer is 'rinses away in water,' which singles out water-soluble PVA rather than any structural filament.
questions.more: Dissolvable support lets you fill tricky internal cavities densely, since you never have to break the support out by hand.
questions.source: Snapmaker, 'What Is PVA Filament' (snapmaker.com); 3Dnatives, 'All You Need to Know About PVA'

questions.cat: 3D printing · balance of toughness vs printability
questions.q: Which filament is prized as a middle ground: stronger and more heat- and chemical-resistant than the easy beginner plastic, yet far less prone to warping than the one that needs an enclosure?
questions.detail: PETG is tougher and more chemical-resistant than PLA but warps far less than ABS, so it prints reliably on open printers.
questions.why: Naming 'the strong one' or 'the easy one' misses it; the slicer is the in-between, and PETG is the filament that splits the difference between PLA and ABS.
questions.more: Good chemical resistance makes PETG popular for bottles, guards, and parts that meet oils or weak acids.
questions.source: Xometry, 'PETG 3D Printing Filament' (xometry.com); Formfutura, 'PETG Filament Material Guide'

questions.cat: 3D printing · many materials in one part
questions.q: One inkjet-style process sprays droplets of liquid resin and cures them on the fly, so a single part can blend several colors and even soft and hard regions at once. Which process is it?
questions.detail: Material jetting (PolyJet) jets tiny drops of photopolymer from inkjet heads and UV-cures them, letting it place different colors and stiffnesses voxel by voxel in one build.
questions.why: The reflex is to say resin printing generally, but only the inkjet droplet method can mix multiple materials per spot; that points to material jetting, not vat SLA or DLP.
questions.more: Voxel-level control lets a single PolyJet part fade from rigid to rubbery without any assembly.
questions.source: Stratasys, 'PolyJet Technology' (stratasys.com); Materialise, 'PolyJet (PJ) 3D Printing'

questions.cat: 3D printing · number of standardized process families
questions.q: International standards sort every additive process into a fixed number of big families, like material extrusion and powder bed fusion. How many families are there?
questions.detail: ISO/ASTM 52900 (from ASTM committee F42) groups all additive manufacturing into seven categories: binder jetting, directed energy deposition, material extrusion, material jetting, powder bed fusion, sheet lamination, and vat photopolymerization.
questions.why: Brand names suggest dozens of methods, but the standard collapses them to a counted set; the answer is the number, and the recognized total is seven.
questions.more: Familiar names map onto these families: FDM is material extrusion, SLA and DLP are vat photopolymerization, and SLS is powder bed fusion.
questions.source: Loughborough University AMRG, 'The 7 categories of Additive Manufacturing'; ISO/ASTM 52900:2021

questions.cat: 3D printing · first-layer helper that does not touch the par
questions.dimension: first-layer helper that does not touch the part
questions.q: On an FDM print, a loop of filament is drawn around the part before it starts, but never touches the part. What is that loop called?
questions.detail: A skirt is a line drawn around the model that does not touch it; it primes the nozzle and lets you check bed level before the real print begins.
questions.why: Brim and raft are tempting, but those both touch the part; the slicer is 'never touches the part,' which only the skirt satisfies.
questions.more: By contrast a brim attaches to the part's edge for grip, and a raft sits underneath the whole part.
questions.source: Simplify3D, 'Rafts, Skirts, and Brims Tutorial'; BCN3D, 'The 3D printing brim, skirt and raft'

questions.q: One metal process is used less to make whole new parts and more to repair worn ones, by melting metal as it is sprayed onto an existing component. Which process is it?
questions.detail: DED melts metal wire or powder as it is fed through a moving nozzle, depositing it onto an existing surface, which makes it ideal for rebuilding worn turbine blades and tools.
questions.why: People expect a powder-bed metal method, but the slicer is 'adds onto an existing part'; that fits DED's nozzle-on-an-arm approach, not a sealed powder bed.
questions.more: Because it builds onto existing geometry, DED also applies wear- and corrosion-resistant coatings, a job called cladding.
questions.source: Loughborough University AMRG, 'Directed Energy Deposition'; RPM Innovations, 'Repair and Cladding DED'

questions.q: Which beginner-friendly filament prints at low heat with no heated bed needed, but will sag and deform if the part is left in a hot car?
questions.detail: PLA prints at modest temperatures and needs no heated bed, but it begins softening around its glass transition near 60 C, so it deforms in a hot car.
questions.why: The trap is to pick a 'weak' plastic generally; the slicer pairs easy printing with a low softening point, which is PLA's signature weakness versus ABS or PETG.
questions.more: Slow heat treating (annealing) PLA can raise its heat resistance somewhat by increasing crystallinity.
questions.source: Simplify3D, 'Tips for 3D Printing with PLA'; Wevolver, 'PLA Melting Point'

questions.q: One whole family of additive machines doesn't melt or cure anything. Instead it stacks sheets of material, glues them down, and cuts each layer to shape. What is this family called?
questions.detail: Sheet lamination (such as Laminated Object Manufacturing) bonds flat sheets, sometimes ordinary paper, then trims each layer's outline with a blade or laser.
questions.why: The reflex is to think every printer melts or cures; the slicer is 'stacks and cuts sheets,' which uniquely names sheet lamination among the families.
questions.more: Because it can use plain paper, sheet lamination was an early cheap way to make full-size, colorable models.
questions.source: Xometry, 'What Is Sheet Lamination in 3D Printing'; Dassault Systemes, 'Sheet Lamination'

questions.q: In a slicer you set how solid the inside of a part is. One popular pattern is a wavy, three-dimensional lattice that is strong in every direction. What is that pattern called?
questions.detail: The gyroid infill is a continuous wavy 3D lattice that gives near-equal strength in all directions, so it can match a denser grid while using less filament.
questions.why: Honeycomb and grid are the easy guesses, but those are flat repeated 2D shapes; the slicer is the wavy 3D, all-directions lattice, which is the gyroid.
questions.more: Setting infill to 0 percent leaves a part hollow and 100 percent makes it solid; the pattern only matters in between.
questions.source: Wevolver, 'Understanding the Gyroid Infill'; Tom's Hardware, 'Which Infill Pattern Should You Use'

questions.cat: 3D printing · fully melting vs partially fusing metal powder
questions.dimension: fully melting vs partially fusing metal powder
questions.q: Two metal powder-bed processes are often confused. One fully melts the metal into a near-solid pool rather than just heating it enough to stick together. Which one fully melts it?
questions.detail: SLM's laser heats metal powder above its melting point into a molten pool that solidifies near fully dense, while sintering-style approaches aim to bond powder below full melting.
questions.why: Both are laser powder-bed metal methods, so the slicer is the word 'fully melts'; that points to SLM by name, even though modern machines blur the line in practice.
questions.more: Full melting is why SLM is favored for dense, load-bearing structural metal parts.
questions.source: Xometry, 'DMLS vs. SLM 3D Printing'; Protolabs, 'DMLS vs. SLM 3D Printing'

questions.q: A Japanese researcher built a working machine that hardened liquid plastic with UV light to grow shapes layer by layer. People assume the technology was born in Silicon Valley in the 1980s. Which decade did he first demonstrate it?
questions.detail: Hideo Kodama of the Nagoya Municipal Industrial Research Institute published a working layer-by-layer photopolymer system in 1981, years before Chuck Hull's 1984 stereolithography patent filing.
questions.why: The reflex is to credit the famous American patent in the mid-1980s, but a Japanese prototype came first, at the start of the decade. The set is small (a few candidate decades) and the answer is older and more eastern than expected.
questions.more: Kodama's full patent lapsed because he missed a filing deadline, so the work was never commercialized and he is often forgotten.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'Hideo Kodama (designer)' and 'Stereolithography'

questions.q: The very first stereolithography patent that launched the industry was filed by an American working at a company that made UV coatings. In which decade was that patent granted?
questions.detail: Chuck Hull filed in 1984 and U.S. patent 4,575,330 was granted in March 1986; he co-founded 3D Systems the same period.
questions.why: Many picture 3D printing as a 2000s-era maker-movement invention because cheap printers only appeared around 2009. Routing to a decade defeats that: the foundational patent is a full generation older.
questions.more: Hull is credited with coining the word 'stereolithography' in his application and also helped create the STL file format.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'Chuck Hull'; USPTO patent US4575330

questions.q: The man who built the first laser-sintering machine, which fuses powder into solid parts, did it as a student project. At which kind of institution was that pioneering work done?
questions.answer_subject: A university (University of Texas at Austin)
questions.state: A university (University of Texas at Austin)
questions.detail: Carl Deckard developed selective laser sintering as a UT Austin student with adviser Joe Beaman; he filed in 1986 and the patent issued in 1990, with DARPA funding.
questions.why: The reflex is a big industrial lab or aerospace contractor. The walkable set (university, corporate R&D, government lab, garage startup) lands on a student thesis project, which surprises.
questions.more: The work was funded partly by DARPA and later spun out into the company DTM, eventually acquired by 3D Systems.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'Selective laser sintering' and 'Carl R. Deckard'

questions.q: One company runs what is widely called the world's largest 3D printing operation, churning out over a million unique parts every day. What everyday product does it actually make?
questions.detail: Align Technology, maker of Invisalign, 3D prints over a million unique molds daily for clear aligners, generally regarded as the largest mass 3D printing operation in the world.
questions.why: Asked for the biggest 3D printing operation, people guess aerospace or automotive. The answer is consumer orthodontics, defeating the heavy-industry reflex, and the field is the slicer.
questions.more: Each patient's teeth are unique, so every aligner mold is a one-off, an ideal fit for additive manufacturing's mass customization.
questions.source: Orthodontic Products, 'Inside Align Technology's Million-a-Day 3D Printing Operation'; Harvard d3 case study

questions.q: One medical device has quietly been built almost entirely by 3D printing for roughly two decades, with nearly the whole industry switching over. Which custom-fit device is it?
questions.detail: Since the early 2000s, in-the-ear hearing aid shells have been almost 100% additively manufactured; Swiss firm Sonova was an early adopter and the industry converted broadly.
questions.why: People think of 3D printing as prototypes or novelties, not a mature mass-production standard. The reflex-defeater is that an entire established medical-device category already runs on it.
questions.more: Each shell is printed from a scan of the wearer's ear canal, so no two are identical, again leveraging mass customization.
questions.source: Sonova, '3D printing technology for improved hearing'; USITC executive brief on 3D printing and hearing aids

questions.q: A famous jet-engine part was redesigned so that around 18 separately made pieces became a single printed piece. What was the new part count for that component?
questions.detail: GE's LEAP engine fuel nozzle tip combined roughly 20 conventional subcomponents into one 3D-printed part, 25% lighter and five times more durable.
questions.why: The question hides the answer by stating the old count (18-ish). The walkable answer is the dramatic endpoint of consolidation, one, which is the whole point of the redesign.
questions.more: Nineteen of these nozzles sit in each LEAP engine; GE has shipped over 100,000 of them from its Auburn, Alabama plant.
questions.source: GE News, 'Transformation in 3D'; 3D Printing Industry

questions.q: A widely cited 'world's first' had an 83-year-old woman receive a complete printed titanium lower jaw. Which country's researchers and manufacturer made it?
questions.detail: The 2011-2012 jaw implant was developed with Hasselt University's biomedical institute and printed by LayerWise, both in Belgium.
questions.why: The reflex guesses the US, Germany, or Japan for a medical-tech first. The set of plausible countries is short, and Belgium is the unexpected, correct slice.
questions.more: The titanium jaw was built by laser-melting powder at about 33 layers per millimeter; the patient could speak again hours after surgery.
questions.source: New Atlas, 'World's first 3D-printed lower jaw implant'; News-Medical

questions.q: The open-source project that aimed to build a 3D printer able to copy its own parts kicked off the cheap-printer boom. In which decade did it begin?
questions.detail: Adrian Bowyer launched the RepRap (self-replicating rapid prototyper) project at the University of Bath in 2004.
questions.why: Hobbyist printers only became common around 2009, so people place the movement's origin then. The seed project is older, sitting earlier in the prior half-decade.
questions.more: RepRap's open designs, released as patents expired, are why desktop FDM printers got cheap; the name means 'replicating rapid prototyper'.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'RepRap'; E3D, 'History of RepRap'

questions.q: The company that built the first commercial 3D printer and the company that pioneered the melted-plastic FDM process became the two giants of the field. Both were founded in the same decade. Which one?
questions.detail: 3D Systems (Chuck Hull) was founded in 1986 and Stratasys (Scott Crump) in 1989, the two dominant early companies.
questions.why: Because consumer 3D printing felt new in the 2010s, people assume the major firms are recent. Routing to a decade reveals both anchor companies are 1980s-born.
questions.more: Scott Crump reportedly conceived FDM trying to make a toy frog for his daughter with a glue gun loaded with plastic and wax.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'Stratasys' and 'S. Scott Crump'

questions.q: The first object ever 3D printed in space was made on the International Space Station. What broad material category was that first space-made part?
questions.detail: The first object printed in space (2014) used extruded plastic; a metal 3D printer only reached the ISS in 2024 with the first metal space part printed in 2024.
questions.why: People may assume space manufacturing means metal tools or parts. The walkable set (plastic, metal, ceramic) lands on humble plastic, with metal arriving a full decade later.
questions.more: The plastic printer was built by the company Made In Space; ESA and Airbus delivered the first metal printer to orbit years afterward.
questions.source: NASA, '3-D Printer Creates First Object in Space'; ESA, 'First metal 3D printing on Space Station'

questions.q: When the industry finally agreed on the formal umbrella name 'additive manufacturing' and set up a standards committee for it, in which decade did that happen?
questions.detail: ASTM Committee F42 on Additive Manufacturing Technologies was formed in 2009, and the F2792 terminology standard followed in 2012.
questions.why: People assume the formal term is old, matching the 1980s tech. In fact standardization lagged by decades, landing surprisingly late relative to the invention.
questions.more: Before F42, vendors used many incompatible names; the committee defined the seven standard process categories still used today.
questions.source: ASTM, 'Committee F42 on Additive Manufacturing Technologies'; 3D Printing Journal

questions.q: The first 3D-printed firearm to actually fire, the Liberator, made global headlines. In which decade was it built and test-fired?
questions.detail: Cody Wilson's Defense Distributed fired the mostly-plastic Liberator in 2013, and blueprints were downloaded over 100,000 times before a takedown order.
questions.why: This one is calibrated against the set's 'older than you think' questions: here the honest answer is genuinely recent, so a player primed to guess early gets caught betting too old.
questions.more: The single metal part in the otherwise plastic gun was a nail used as a firing pin; the State Department forced the files offline.
questions.source: V&A Museum, 'The Liberator'; TIME

questions.q: The fuel nozzle that made GE famous in 3D printing is built by fusing metal powder with a laser. Which broad process family does that belong to?
questions.detail: GE's metal nozzles are made by laser powder bed fusion, melting fine metal powder layer by layer in a bed.
questions.why: People may guess 'metal printing' generically or confuse it with binder spraying. The walkable set of named process families resolves to powder bed fusion, the laser-melt category.
questions.more: GE acquired Concept Laser and Arcam to control this metal process; the same family makes orthopedic implants and rocket parts.
questions.source: GE Aerospace, manufacturing milestone; 3D Printing Industry

questions.q: Early desktop 3D printers suddenly became affordable around 2009 largely because a key invention's legal protection ran out. Roughly how many years does a US patent's protection last, which sets that clock?
questions.detail: US utility patents last about 20 years from filing; FDM patents from the late 1980s expiring around 2009 enabled cheap RepRap-derived printers.
questions.why: Players know patents expire but rarely the exact term. The count is a small, bounded number, and 20 explains the 2009 timing without the question stating it.
questions.more: The expiry of Stratasys's FDM patents is widely credited with launching the consumer desktop printer market.
questions.source: USPTO patent term rules; Wikipedia, 'Fused filament fabrication'
